original_hypothesis.title: CoinbaseSmartWalletFactory createAccount salt depends on raw owners bytes ordering; duplicate semantic owner sets can create multiple addresses, breaking user expectations and recovery tooling
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Factory salt is keccak256(abi.encode(owners, nonce)) where owners is a bytes[] array. The same set of owners in different order yields different wallet addresses; additionally, different byte encodings that are semantically equivalent (e.g., address(0) vs malformed 32 bytes within uint160 range) may produce different addresses. This can be exploited to confuse users or integrators into deploying 'lookalike' wallets and splitting funds.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Not a direct on-chain theft, but a practical operational attack surface: if off-chain systems assume owner set is order-independent, an attacker can induce users to fund an unintended address with same owners but different ordering/encoding. Given the constraints, this is more of a correctness/UX risk but can lead to loss of funds if users deposit to the wrong address.
deployment_assumptions: Integrations compute or display predicted addresses from owner sets without canonical sorting/normalization
Users or apps might fund the predicted address assuming set semantics rather than ordered list semantics
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No documentation states owners are sorted/canonicalized; factory uses raw abi.encode of owners array.

proof_of_signal.pattern: salt derived from ordered, non-canonical bytes[] input

preconditions: Victim/integration relies on predicted address from owners set
Attacker can influence ordering/encoding presented to victim (e.g., via API suggestions, client-side bugs, or multi-platform mismatch)
Victim funds the wrong derived address

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: no canonicalization of owner list before address derivation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: address derivation ambiguity; potential fund loss via confusion
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no sorting, no normalization, no unique set enforcement at factory level

analysis_questions: Do official clients sort/normalize owners before calling getAddress/createAccount?
Are there multiple encodings accepted by initialize/_initializeOwners that represent the same owner?
Is there any UI/API that claims address depends only on set, not order?
validation_requirements: Demonstrate two different owner orderings produce two different addresses
Check whether MultiOwnable accepts multiple encodings for same address owner (should not, but verify encoding constraints end-to-end)

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker convinces a user/integration to compute a wallet address using owners in a different order than the one used when deploying (or uses a different client that orders differently). User sends funds to the mismatched predicted address; funds are controlled by same owners but may not be monitored/recovered by tooling expecting the canonical address.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: User funds misdirected to an unintended account address; recovery depends on owners noticing and accessing it
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users using multiple platforms/integrations to derive wallet addresses
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Possible if owners control both accounts, but operationally error-prone

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests asserting canonical owner ordering requirements or documenting them

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: identify multiple deployed wallets sharing same owner set in different orders

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
